title: Building Bigger Decisions from Gates
concept: Simple gates like AND, OR, and NOT can be wired together so one gate's output feeds another gate's input, turning tiny true/false rules into far more complex decisions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: An AND gate outputs true only when...

options.text: Every one of its inputs is true
At least one input is true
None of its inputs are true

exercises.explain: Exactly! AND is the strict gate — everything has to check out. Today we start combining gates like that into bigger decisions.

cards.title: One lock is rarely enough
cards.body: A bank vault doesn't just check one thing. It checks a keycard, AND a PIN, AND sometimes a manager's approval too. No single gate could capture a rule that strict — so engineers just chain gates together.

cards.title: Combining gates
cards.body: You can combine gates by feeding the output of one gate into the input of another. This turns a handful of simple true/false rules into one much more complex decision — without inventing any new kind of logic.

cards.title: Logic gates are like LEGO bricks
cards.body: No single LEGO brick makes a castle. But stack enough simple bricks together in the right pattern, and you get something complex. Gates work the same way — the complexity comes from combination, not from any single gate being smart.

cards.title: A layered security system
cards.body: "Unlock the vault" = keycard AND PIN. "Override the vault" = (manager present OR emergency code entered) AND keycard. Two small rules, wired together, cover a much richer real-world policy.

cards.title: Complex doesn't mean a new kind of rule
cards.body: It's tempting to think a complicated decision — like a bank's whole security policy — needs some fundamentally smarter kind of logic. It doesn't. It's still just AND, OR, and NOT, wired together in layers.

left.text:
One rule, one decision
e.g. "has a keycard"

right.text:
Many rules chained together
e.g. "keycard AND PIN AND (manager OR code)"

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best describes COMBINING logic gates?

options.text: A door unlocks only if you have a keycard AND you enter the correct PIN
A light turns on whenever it gets dark
A single switch that is either on or off
Flipping a true value to false

exercises.explain: Right! That rule combines two separate checks (keycard, PIN) with an AND gate — that's combination in action.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Combining gates requires inventing a brand-new type of logic beyond AND, OR, and NOT.
exercises.explain: False. Combining gates just wires the same three basic gates together — no new kind of logic is needed.

exercises.sentence: To combine gates, you feed the ___ of one gate into the input of another.
exercises.explain: A gate's output becomes the next gate's input — that's exactly how chains of gates are built.

exercises.prompt: Which real-world rule is an example of gates being COMBINED, not just one gate acting alone?

options.text: You can drive if you have a license AND the car has gas AND you are sober
A motion sensor turns on one light by itself

exercises.explain: Correct! Three separate checks — license, gas, sober — are all ANDed together, which is combined logic.

exercises.prompt: A school computer lab door unlocks if a student has a valid ID AND (it is during lab hours OR a teacher swipes an override badge). Which combination of gates best models this rule?

options.text: An OR gate (lab hours OR override) feeding into an AND gate with the ID check
A single AND gate with only the ID and lab hours, ignoring the override
There's no way to model this rule with gates

exercises.explain: Exactly! The OR gate's output becomes one input to the AND gate — a small chain of gates capturing a real, layered rule. Next: how do we prove a gate behaves exactly as expected for every possible input? That's the truth table.
